audit_trail.notes: Three reviewers completed two full cycles. Gemini cycle 1 was substantially more generous (7.0/10) than claude and codex (~5.4–5.5); by cycle 2 gemini accepted codex's points on the X→0 late-time issue and branch selection, downgrading to 5.0. All three reviewers converged on the undemonstrated interpolation as the central unresolved gap. Mechanical score selected from cycle 2 (5.333 average). Claude maintained 'promising' verdict; codex maintained 'contested'; gemini moved to 'weak_evidence'. The paper was also associated with a Sabine Hossenfelder YouTube video ('Action At A Distance Can Explain Dark Matter Physicists Show') as the source of inclusion in this review batch.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper by Deffayet and Woodard proposes a modified gravity theory — requiring no new particles — that the authors argue can simultaneously account for why galaxies rotate the way they do (usually attributed to dark matter) and reproduce the large-scale cosmological imprints of dark matter. The review board found the individual building blocks credible, since each was established in prior published work by the same group, but unanimously agreed that the paper's central new claim — that the two regimes are connected by a single smooth mathematical equation — has not been demonstrated even in a simplified toy calculation. The most important caveat is that a key quantity in the model appears to approach zero near the present cosmic epoch, which could undermine the very cosmological equivalence the model claims to provide.
human_spotchecks: Verify whether ref [14] (Tan & Woodard 2018, JCAP, arXiv:1804.01669) actually performs a perturbative stability analysis for the specific nonlocal MOND model defined by eqs. (27)–(30) in Section 3 of this paper, or only for an earlier variant of the model class. The paper asserts stability has been 'conducted with the same result' for the MOND model of Section 4, citing [14], but Section 4 introduces the interpolating eq. (33) which is not in [14].

Determine the quantitative consequences of the cosmological invariant X→0 at late times (z≈0, Appendix 7, citing ref [13], arXiv:1608.07858): does this near-zero crossing cause f(X) to grow to non-negligible values during late-time structure formation, and has ref [13] assessed whether this creates tension with background expansion, growth rate, or CMB lensing data for the synthesized model?

Survey whether the nonlocal MOND model of refs [11,12] (Deffayet, Esposito-Farese & Woodard 2011/2014) has been independently tested against cluster-scale phenomenology, N-body structure formation, or the diversity of disk galaxy rotation curve shapes, beyond the BTFR and isolated static weak lensing for which it was constructed.

Assess whether the branch-selection problem for coupled nonlocal equations analogous to eq. (33) has a known treatment in the nonlocal modified gravity literature (e.g., Maggiore-Mancarella, Deser-Woodard earlier work on □⁻¹R models), and whether attractor analysis, stability criteria, or initial-condition sensitivity of solution-branch selection have been established for such equations.

Check whether a full linearized tensor-mode analysis of the complete synthesized nonlocal action (eq. 31 with the boundary prescription of eq. 27) confirms that gravitational wave propagation speed and friction are unmodified around both cosmological and mixed matter-dominated backgrounds, accounting for the nonlocal memory in X from background curvature history.
public_summary: Deffayet and Woodard present a nonlocal modification of general relativity intended to unify two previously separate lines of phenomenological work: a cosmological model that mimics cold dark matter by expressing its conserved stress tensor as a nonlocal functional of the metric, and a relativistic MOND model that reproduces flat galactic rotation curves and weak lensing without dark matter. The synthesis is achieved through a single nonlocal Lagrangian addition (eq. 31) governed by an interpolating equation (eq. 33) for a functional F. The interpolating function f(X), where X is built from a nonlocal contraction of the Ricci tensor, is designed to be negligible when X is large and negative (cosmological regime) and to activate the MOND correction when spatial metric gradients dominate (bound system regime). The paper also presents a recursive proof that the continuum 4-velocity field does not form caustics in a static spherically symmetric geometry, in contrast to the point-particle representation. Both ingredients (the cosmological sector and the MOND sector) are inherited from prior publications by the same authors; the novel contribution is the unified Lagrangian and the conjecture that eq. (33) smoothly interpolates between regimes. The paper is transparent that a quantitative demonstration of the transition mechanism is deferred to future numerical work, making the central new result a well-motivated framework proposal rather than a completed demonstration.
reviewer_disagreements: Verdict disagreement across cycles: claude (both cycles) recommended 'promising'; codex recommended 'contested' (both cycles); gemini downgraded from 'promising' (cycle 1, score 7.0) to 'weak_evidence' (cycle 2, score 5.0) after accepting the X→0 and branch-selection points raised by other reviewers.

Clarity scoring diverged: gemini cycle 1 gave 9/10, arguing the paper is clearly structured and physically well-motivated; claude gave 7/10, citing heavy reliance on unexplained equation-numbering from prior papers and loss of equation content in HTML rendering; codex gave 5.2–6.0, also noting rendering issues and assumed familiarity with the prior model series.

Claude and codex disagreed on whether the 'automatic' cosmological CDM reproduction constitutes a major objection: codex labeled it a major flaw (the final model changes F, so success is not automatic); claude partially rejected this, noting the paper's stated design intent, but accepted the refined version that the final synthesized model's perturbation theory needs independent checking.

Gemini cycle 1 labeled both the cosmological and gravitationally bound claims as 'well_supported' without noting these are entirely inherited from prior publications; claude and codex consistently labeled them at most 'partially_supported' for the synthesized model, since eq. (33) has not been verified to preserve either limit in realistic settings.

Significance scoring: gemini cycle 1 gave 8/10 and codex gave 7.0–7.2, reflecting the potential importance if validated; claude gave 7/10, which it maintained, arguing that significance should reflect demonstrated rather than hoped-for results but acknowledging the genuine importance of the problem addressed.

strongest_objections.objection: The claimed smooth interpolation between the cosmological and MOND regimes (eq. 33) is not demonstrated quantitatively. Section 4.2 explicitly acknowledges that 'a definitive analysis in a realistic setting is obviously a formidable numerical undertaking' and proposes only a future toy 2D study. The paper asserts that spatial metric gradient dominance 'pushes F toward the MOND solution' but provides no calculation, dimensional estimate, branch-selection analysis, or even a schematic toy example for any realistic transition geometry. The central new result of the paper is therefore a conjecture with correct asymptotic design, not a demonstrated result.

Appendix 7 shows that the cosmological invariant X→0 at late cosmic times (approximately z≈0 today), weakening the suppression of f(X) in the current epoch. When X is near zero, the MOND correction is not suppressed, potentially reactivating it during late-time cosmology precisely where precision observations constrain the background expansion, growth of structure, and CMB lensing. The paper does not assess whether this creates observational tension and defers to ref [13] without analysis in the context of the synthesized model.

Eq. (33) has (at least) two solution branches: a homogeneous cosmological branch and an inhomogeneous MOND branch. The mechanism by which a galaxy or cluster selects the MOND inhomogeneous branch rather than remaining on the cosmological homogeneous branch is not analyzed. The paper does not establish whether the transition is driven by an attractor instability of one branch, by the magnitude of spatial gradients relative to initial conditions, or some other mechanism. Without this, the synthesis claim cannot be evaluated.

Eq. (33) modifies the functional F relative to the pure dark-matter construction of ref [15]. The paper claims cosmological success is automatic because the model 'is just dark matter,' but this applies to the Section 2 model only. The final synthesized model's linear cosmological perturbation theory — whether f(X) suppression is quantitatively sufficient across all redshifts and scales relevant to CMB, BAO, and structure formation — has not been verified.

The stability citations ([47] for mimetic gravity, [14] for the MOND sector) cover the two limiting models in isolation. The synthesized model, coupling both sectors through the interpolating equation (33) and f(X), has not been analyzed perturbatively. New ghost modes or instabilities can appear precisely in the transition regime between two otherwise stable configurations.

Galactic cluster phenomenology — including the Bullet Cluster and cluster core mass discrepancies, traditionally regarded as severe challenges for MOND — is acknowledged as problematic but entirely deferred. The suggestion that 'competition between the cosmological and MOND solutions may account for these systems' is speculative and unsupported by any calculation.

The tensor mode propagation argument for the MOND sector (source contains two Ricci tensor factors, which vanish for gravitational radiation) is correct locally but insufficient for the full nonlocal model. The nonlocal operator □⁻¹ in the definition of X (eq. 27) accumulates contributions from all past curvature history including matter-dominated epochs; a propagating gravitational wave in a cosmological background will encounter a nonzero X from background contributions, and the quadratic action for tensor perturbations of the complete synthesized model has not been derived.

The numerical coincidence a₀ ≈ cH₀/2π (Milgrom's constant ≈ Hubble acceleration scale) is used in eq. (10) as a load-bearing input that allows both the cosmological and MOND regimes to work simultaneously. The paper explicitly labels it a 'numerical coincidence' and provides no dynamical mechanism to explain or stabilize it. This is a significant unexplained fine-tuning in the synthesis.

strongest_supported_claims: The cosmological sector (eqs. 5 and 9) is by construction equivalent to a separately conserved pressureless dark matter stress tensor at linear perturbation order, so it reproduces CMB anisotropies, BAO, and linear structure formation to the same precision as CDM within the scope of the prior model [15]; this follows definitionally from the conservation constraint used to define the stress tensor.

The gravitationally bound sector reproduces the Baryonic Tully-Fisher Relation and the weak lensing condition Φ=Ψ in the static spherically symmetric weak-field limit, by algebraic construction from eq. (20) and the static Einstein equations; these results are supported by prior validation in refs [11, 12].

In the deep cosmological regime at high redshift, the invariant X is large and negative (proven analytically in Appendix 7), making f(X) infinitesimally small and suppressing the MOND correction, consistent with the cosmological limit of the model.

The continuum 4-velocity field (gradient of scalar φ) does not form caustics at r=0 in a static spherically symmetric geometry: Appendix 6 gives a clean recursive proof that all radial derivatives of φ vanish at the origin for all time, distinguishing the field-theory representation from the point-particle representation where caustics do occur.

The paper demonstrates that the gravitational wave propagation speed constraint from multimessenger observations (GW170817) is satisfied by the MOND sector of the model, because the MOND source term contains two factors of the Ricci tensor, which vanishes for gravitational radiation; the cosmological sector satisfies this constraint by analogy with Barvinsky's mimetic gravity analysis [47].
technical_takeaway: The paper synthesizes a prior nonlocal mimetic-gravity CDM construction (Deffayet & Woodard 2024, ref [15]) and a prior nonlocal MOND realization (Deffayet, Esposito-Farese & Woodard 2011/2014, refs [11,12]) into a single Lagrangian via a proposed interpolating equation (eq. 33), governed by a function f(X) where X is a nonlocal Ricci-tensor invariant. Each limiting regime is sound by prior construction: the cosmological sector is definitionally equivalent to a conserved pressureless dark matter stress tensor at linear order, and the gravitationally bound sector reproduces the BTFR and lensing condition Φ=Ψ in the static spherically symmetric limit. Three reviewers converge on two major unresolved issues across both review cycles: (1) the interpolation mechanism is entirely qualitative — eq. (33) has correct asymptotic design but no quantitative demonstration, toy geometry solution, or branch-selection analysis in a realistic transition spacetime exists; and (2) the cosmological invariant X→0 at late times (z≈0, Appendix 7), potentially reactivating the MOND correction during current cosmic epochs and undermining the CDM mimicry the model relies upon. Stability of the synthesized interpolating model, nonlinear structure formation in the transition regime, and cluster-scale phenomenology are all deferred to future work.
